subject: Social and Political Life - II
chapter_name: Growing Up as Boys and Girls
overall_learning_objectives: Understand that growing up experiences are different for boys and girls in different societies
Learn about the gender differences through stories of Samoa and Madhya Pradesh
Recognize that society values the work of boys and girls differently
Understand that housework is 'invisible' and often undervalued
Identify the role of 'care-giving' in a family
Learn about the lives of domestic workers
Understand the government's role in promoting equality (e.g., Anganwadis and Crèches)

lesson_plan.title: Growing Up in Different Societies
topics_covered: The story of Samoa Islands in the 1920s
How children learned to look after siblings and fish
Growing up in Madhya Pradesh in the 1960s (Separate schools)
teaching_strategy: Storytelling: Life on an island without schools
Comparison: Walking home from school (Boys playing vs. Girls walking in groups)
Visual aids showing different school layouts
student_activities: List 2 things Samoan children did instead of going to school
Discuss: 'Why did girls in MP walk in groups?'
assessment: Oral quiz: At what age did Samoan boys join older boys for fishing?
Identify one difference between boys' and girls' schools in the 1960s MP story

lesson_plan.title: Valuing Boys and Girls
topics_covered: How toys teach us gender roles (Cars for boys, Dolls for girls)
Society's expectations from men and women
The link between toys and future careers
teaching_strategy: Discussion: 'What toys did you play with as a child?'
Activity: Sorting 'Boys' toys' and 'Girls' toys' and questioning why
Explaining that toys are a way of telling children they have different futures
student_activities: Write one thing people say boys should do and one thing girls should do
assessment: Short answer: Why are girls often given dolls to play with?
True/False: Society treats boys and girls the same way from childhood

lesson_plan.title: Valuing Housework
topics_covered: The story of Hermeet's mother (Harsharan's family)
A day without housework: What happens?
Housework as 'Real Work'
teaching_strategy: Reading the comic strip 'My Mother does not work'
Role-play: A morning in a house where no one does the chores
Explaining that housework is time-consuming and physically demanding
student_activities: List 5 tasks your mother/father does at home every day
Calculate: If a person works from 5 AM to 10 PM at home, how many hours is that?
assessment: Oral quiz: Is housework easy or difficult?

lesson_plan.title: Lives of Domestic Workers
topics_covered: The story of Melani
Working hours and conditions of domestic helpers
Lack of respect for 'Care-giving' work
teaching_strategy: Reading Melani’s experience of eating 'dry rotis'
Discussion on why domestic workers are paid less
Highlighting the physical labor: sweeping, washing, cooking
student_activities: Write a 'Thank You' note to someone who helps clean your house or school
Discuss: 'Why do we think domestic work is not valuable?'
assessment: Short answer: Who is a domestic worker?
Identify two problems faced by domestic workers

lesson_plan.title: Women’s Work and Equality
topics_covered: The 'Double Burden' of women's work
Equality as a part of the Indian Constitution
Why the government needs to step in
teaching_strategy: Analogy: Carrying two bags (Home work + Outside work)
Explaining that inequality is a 'system' and not just a family issue
Discussion on Article 15 (No discrimination based on sex)
student_activities: Draw a picture of the 'Double Burden'
Identify if the Constitution allows treating girls differently
assessment: True/False: Does the government care about equality?

lesson_plan.title: Government Steps for Equality
topics_covered: Anganwadis (Childcare centres)
The Law on Crèches in organizations
How these help girls attend school
teaching_strategy: Explaining what an Anganwadi does
Discussion: 'If a mother has a crèche at work, how does it help the older daughter?'
Visuals of a village Anganwadi
student_activities: Find out if there is an Anganwadi near your house
Write 2 benefits of having a crèche at a workplace
assessment: Short answer: What is a Crèche?
How do Anganwadis help women work outside the home?

lesson_plan.title: Revision and Summary
topics_covered: Recap: Samoa vs. MP
Recap: Valuing housework and domestic workers
Government measures
teaching_strategy: Fun game: 'Task Master' (sorting home tasks vs. office tasks)
Reviewing NCERT back-exercises
Final doubt-clearing session
student_activities: Participate in the 'Equal Rights' class quiz
Complete the summary table: Role of Boy/Girl then vs. now

teaching_aids: Social and Political Life - II Textbook
Pictures of Samoa Islands and 1960s schools
Video clips of an Anganwadi in action
evaluation_methods: Matching columns: Work vs. Its Value
teacher_tip: Students at this level might think housework is 'nothing' because they see it every day. Use the 'One Day Strike' idea from the textbook to help them realize how the house would stop functioning without that work.
